Paper 1::Sociocultural::SAQs.title: Explain Social Identity Theory with reference to one study.
Paper 1::Sociocultural::SAQs.content:
Introduction


Social Identity Theory (SIT), proposed by Tajfel and Turner (1979), explains how individuals drive part of their self-concept from the social groups to which they belong.

According to the theory, people first engage in social categorization, classifying themselves and others into groups, distinguishing between Ã¢ÂÂin-groupsÃ¢ÂÂ (us) and Ã¢ÂÂout-groupsÃ¢ÂÂ (them).

This is followed by social identification, where individuals adopt the norms, values, and behaviour of their in-group. 

Once group membership is established, individuals engage in social comparison, evaluating their in-group relative to out-groups in order to justify their group affiliation. 

Through this process, people seek positive distinctiveness, enhancing their self-esteem by favouring their in-group over out-groups, often leading to in-group favouritism and out-group discrimination.


Study: Tajfel and Turner

Although the boys did not initially choose their groups, the researchers' arbitrary assignment of in-groups demonstrates social categorization, as participants quickly distinguished between Ã¢ÂÂusÃ¢ÂÂ and Ã¢ÂÂthem.Ã¢ÂÂ 

Despite the lack of meaningful differences, the boys began associating primarily with members of their assigned group, illustrating social identification, whereby they perceived themselves as similar and bonded through shared group membership, leading to the formation of group norms. 

The boys then engaged in social comparison, evaluating their in-group more favourably than the out-group and perceiving the other group as inferior. 

This comparison manifested in out-group discrimination, as participants attempted to minimize the points awarded to the opposing group in the second reward system.

Simultaneously, positive distinctiveness was achieved through in-group favoritism, with participants maximizing rewards for their own group in the first reward system.

Overall, these findings support Social Identity Theory by demonstrating that even minimal and meaningless social groupings are sufficient to produce in-group favoritism and out-group discrimination, independent of real competition or conflict.

Paper 1::Sociocultural::SAQs.title: Explain Social Cognitive Theory with reference to one study.
Paper 1::Sociocultural::SAQs.content:
Introduction


Social Cognitive Theory (SCT), developed by Bandura, explains how people learn behaviours by observing others. Social cognitive theory can be broken down into several different aspects but consists mostly of observational learning, that is, the ability to develop new behaviors from the viewing of a modelâs behavior and the imitation of said behaviors.

The theory highlights four key cognitive factors: attention, retention, motivation, and potential. 

Attention refers to the selective choice of what to observe as compared to ignoring other actions. 

Retention is the maintenance of the aforementioned said observations to possible use in a future situation. 

Motivation is the desire to perform a specific behavior. 
Without a need for the behavior, no imitation is likely leading to eventual loss of the attitude. 

Finally, reproduction/potential is the capability to perform the action both mentally and physically. 

To âreproduceâ an action, one must be able to do so and it must be within their limitations. 

For observational learning to occur, the model must first stand out from others to capture attention. 

Their behavior should be consistent, making it easy for the observer to understand and imitate. 

The model must also be respected, so their actions are perceived as meaningful and worth copying. 

Additionally, the model should be similar to the observer, which increases motivation and retention, as seeing someone similar succeed encourages imitation.


Study: Kimball


The findings can be explained using Social Cognitive Theory (SCT), as the children were influenced by models presented on television.

Because these models shared similar cultural backgrounds, children were more likely to pay attention to them, allowing gender-based beliefs portrayed in the media to be observed and internalized.

Boys appeared to be more affected, as male characters are more frequently represented on television, increasing the likelihood that boys attend to imitate these models. 

The children were also able to retain these observed beliefs and were motivated to reproduce them, as they possessed the physical and cognitive ability to do so. 

Overall, this demonstrates SCT, showing that children can acquire cultural norms and gender stereotypes simply through observing media models even without direct personal interaction.

Paper 1::Sociocultural::SAQs.title: Explain one theory of the formation of stereotypes with reference to one study.
Paper 1::Sociocultural::SAQs.content:
Introduction


stereotype is a social perception of an individual based on generalized assumptions about their group membership or physical attributes.

These generalizations can be either positive or negative; for example, women may be stereotyped as skilled communicators, or conversely, as poor drivers.

Stereotypes are often acquired through socialization, such as influences from parents, peers, and broader social norms reinforced by the media, as well as through personal experience, yet they cannot be accurately applied to all members of a group.

Cognitively, stereotypes function as schemas – mental frameworks based on preconceived notions that help individuals quickly interpret and organize information about the social world.

The development and maintenance of stereotypes can be explained by Social Cognitive Theory, which proposes that humans learn behaviors, attitudes, and beliefs by overseeing and imitating others, known as models.

When a model is rewarded or punished for a behaviour, observers may be encouraged or discouraged from replicating it, a process known as vicarious reinforcement.

Imitation is more probable when the model displays consistent behaviour, is perceived as similar to the observer, and is seen as likable or admirable.

Additionally, the observer's level of self-efficacy plays a crucial role; individuals with high self-efficacy - confidence in their ability to succeed - are more willing to imitate behaviors and persist through challenges, whereas those with low self-efficacy may avoid imitation due to fear of failure.

SCT has four constructs associated with the theory:

Attention: The observer must pay attention to the model. Whether or not one pays attention to them depends on factors, such as the model’s attractiveness or authority.

Retention: Observer must be able to remember behaviour that has been observed in order to imitate it immediately or soon after.

Motivation: Observer must be able to want to imitate the behaviour, having to understand the consequences of performing that behaviour in the process.

Potential: Observer must be able to do this behaviour, physically or mentally, having enough self-efficacy to do so.


Study: Kimball


The children were influenced by models on TV, according to Social Cognitive Theory.

By attending to television models who share a similar cultural background—making them more salient and relatable—children were more likely to absorb and internalize the gender-based beliefs being portrayed.

Boys were more affected, as males tend to be on TV more often, and thus boys may pay more attention and imitate the models more.

Children were clearly able to retain these beliefs, motivating them to imitate these beliefs, which they easily have the physical and mental potential to do.

Stereotypes are perpetuated by the gender roles observed by the children on television.

Paper 1::Sociocultural::SAQs.title: Explain one study of the effects of stereotyping on behaviour.
Paper 1::Sociocultural::SAQs.content:
Introduction


A stereotype is a social perception of an individual based on generalized assumptions about their group membership or physical attributes.

These generalizations can be either positive or negative; for example, women may be stereotyped as skilled communicators, or conversely, as poor drivers.

Stereotypes are often acquired through socialization, such as influences from parents, peers, and broader social norms reinforced by the media, as well as through personal experience, yet they cannot be accurately applied to all members of a group.

Cognitively, stereotypes function as schemas – mental frameworks based on preconceived notions that help individuals quickly interpret and organize information about the social world.

One effect of stereotypes is the stereotype threat, which is when one is in a situation where they are being treated stereotypically, or of doing something that would reaffirm these stereotypes.

One does not have to believe in the stereotype for it to affect their behaviour. It turns on spotlight anxiety, which is when one fears being watched and judged by others. This causes emotional distress and pressure that can undermine performance.

Individuals worry about appearing to validate negative stereotypes concerning social groups to which they belong.

Due to this anxiety, there may be an avoidance of behaviour that would believe would affirm these stereotypes.

Study: Steele & Aronson


Stereotype threat could be observed here. African Americans are a heavily stereotyped race in America, so the fear of reinforcing those stereotypes led them to create emotional pressure.

This uses up cognitive resources and the focus on culture, made more salient via the questionnaire, and reduced their performance.

Paper 1::Sociocultural::SAQs.title: Explain one ethical consideration in one study of the individual and the group.
Paper 1::Sociocultural::SAQs.content:
Introduction


One important ethical consideration in studies examining the individual and the group is The Right to Withdraw.

Participants must be able to discontinue their involvement at any time if they no longer feel comfortable or safe, without facing pressure or negative consequences.

This ethical safeguard prevents individuals from feeling forced or trapped in a research situation and reduces the risk of undue stress.

To ensure this right is upheld, researchers should clearly emphasize it throughout the study, including in written consent forms and through clear verbal or written reminders that participation is voluntary.

Participants should also be allowed to skip questions, particularly in questionnaires or interviews that may involve sensitive topics.

This consideration is especially important in the individual and the group, as studies may involve sensitive social identities, group membership, or situations that create psychological pressure to conform or continue participating.

If a participant wishes to withdraw, researchers must respect this decision to protect participant well-being and uphold ethical standards.

Study: Kimball


The right to withdraw is particularly relevant in Kimball’s study, as the research involved children and addressed sensitive topics related to gender role and stereotyping which may cause discomfort for both participants and their parents.

Because children are a vulnerable population, parents may become uncomfortable with researchers questioning their children about gender expectations or social behaviour, especially if they feel the study could influence their children’s beliefs or portray them negatively.

Additionally, the results of the study suggest that exposure to television led to an increase in gender stereotyping among children, particularly boys.

Parents may feel that these findings place their child or community in a negative light and may therefore choose to withdraw their data to avoid association with these outcomes. Respecting this decision is essential to prevent participants from feeling pressured or coerced into continued participation.

By allowing parents and children to withdraw at any point without negative consequences, researchers protect participant well-being and maintain ethical standards.

This ensures that participation remains voluntary throughout the longitudinal study and upholds trust and transparency between researchers and participants, which is especially important in research involving the individual and the group.

Paper 1::Sociocultural::SAQs.title: Explain the use of one research method in one study of the individual and the group.
Paper 1::Sociocultural::SAQs.content:
Introduction


One research method used in the study of the individual and the group is the natural experiment.

A natural experiment is characterized by researchers studying naturally occurring changes in an independent variable (IV) rather than manipulating it themselves.

The IV is not controlled by the researcher, but it still affects a dependent variable (DV).

The DV is operationalized, meaning that it is measured numerically or categorically using a scoring or categorization system devised by the researchers.

Because the IV cannot be manipulated, researchers have less control over extraneous variables, and conditions cannot always be fully standardized.

Participants may still be compared across groups exposed to different levels of the IV, but allocation is not random - groups exist naturally.

Procedures may be standardized where possible, but tasks are often realistic or naturally occurring rather than artificial.

Natural experiments are used when it is impossible, impractical, or unethical to manipulate the IV directly, allowing researchers to investigate causal relationships in real-world settings, though with less control than true experiments.

Study: Kimball


Kimball’s study provides empirical evidence for the use of a natural experiment in the study of the individual and the group.

The IV was naturally occurring with the introduction to TV in the NOTEL condition, whereas the DV was the formation of gender stereotypes, operationalized via self-reported data.

The IV was dependent on the # of TV stations, and was not manipulated by researchers.

The researchers did not provide a task; they simply took baseline measurements before leaving the child for 2 years before taking another measurement. Thus, this is not a standardized controlled environment, as every child watches different channels or has different lives.

It is unfeasible to introduce TV to every area, so researchers relied on TV being introduced naturally and taking advantage of that opportunity.

It is also ethically questionable to directly and intentionally influence a developing child’s gender stereotypes in a potentially permanent manner.

Paper 1::Sociocultural::SAQs.title: Explain one cultural dimension with reference to one study.
Paper 1::Sociocultural::SAQs.content:
Introduction


According to Matsumoto, culture is “the set of attitudes, values, beliefs, and behaviors shared by a group of people, but different for each individual, communicating from one generation to the next.”

In other words, being in cultural groups can influence cognition and create new behaviours.

Cultural norms (the unwritten rules, shared expectations, and behaviors considered normal or appropriate within a specific society or group) and values are communicated to children, creating new schemas and affecting what we remember.

One way culture affects cognition is by affecting memory, the process of encoding and retrieving information.

This influence can also be done through cultural dimensions, which are the trends of behaviour in a given culture. For example, the extent to which a culture respects authority, tolerance for change, masculinity/feminity, attitudes towards tradition, and indulgence. There are 6 dimensions.

One dimension in particular, individualism vs collectivism, plays a role in influencing behaviour and cognition (such as emotional memory formation). In individualistic cultures, individual achievement, uniqueness, freedom, and self-reliance are important. Conversely in collectivist cultures, social harmony, group membership, modesty, the interests of the group, and interdependence, are valued.

Study: Kulkofsky


Provides direct empirical evidence for the role of a core cultural dimension  (individualism vs collectivism) in shaping cognition and downstream behaviour.

They found that personal importance and emotional intensity predicted flashbulb memory formation more strongly in individualistic countries (Germany, Turkey, UK, USA) than in China, whereas national importance predicted FBMs equally across cultures.

This pattern illustrates the mechanism by which cultural dimensions affect behaviour: cultural values bias what people attend to and rehearse.

Collectivist cultures, more focused on social harmony and group status, are less likely to focus on themselves as individuals, so they would not rehearse events more personally important to them, and rather focus on nationally important events.

Individualistic cultures are more focused on themselves, thus more personal events are more likely to be vividly remembered (and confirms importance driven model of FBM theory).

Paper 1::Sociocultural::SAQs.title: Explain one study of the enculturation of one behaviour.
Paper 1::Sociocultural::SAQs.content:
Introduction


Enculturation is the process in which an individual internalizes the social norms, values, and beliefs of their own culture into their identity.

According to Matsumoto, culture is “the set of attitudes, values, beliefs, and behaviours shared by a group of people, but different for each individual, communicated from one generation to the next.”

In other words, different groups have different cultural norms, transferring through each generation. Although enculturation is a lifelong process, it starts at infancy and childhood, as young children learn new information very quickly.

Social Cognitive Theory can explain how enculturation occurs.

According to Social Cognitive Theory, learning from a model occurs through key processes.

First, the observer must pay attention to the model, which is more likely if the model has consistent behaviour, is similar to the observer in age, gender, or nationality, or is generally likable.

Next, the observer must retain the behaviour by remembering it in order to imitate it immediately or later.

Then, the observer must have the potential to perform the behaviour, which depends on their physical or mental ability and their level of self-efficacy (the belief that they can successfully complete a task).

Observers with high self-efficacy are more likely to attempt new behaviours, overcome challenges, and recover from setbacks, whereas those with low self-efficacy and fear of failure are less likely to imitate.

Finally, the observer must have motivation to replicate behaviours, norms, and values that are often modeled by influential or familiar members of a cultural group, and observers selectively imitate those behaviours that are consistent with their cultural context.

This process helps explain cultural learning, as behaviours, norms, and values are often modeled by influential or familiar members of a cultural group, and observers selectively imitate those behaviours that are consistent with their cultural context, remembered, possible for them to perform, and socially or personally rewarded.

Study: Kimball


The children were influenced by models on TV, according to Social Cognitive Theory (SCT).

Paying attention to the models on TV, which have a similar culture to the children and thus more likely for them to pay attention to, children were able to pick up on gender-based beliefs that were broadcasted.

Boys were more affected, as males tend to be on TV more often, and thus boys may pay more attention and imitate the models more.

Children were clearly able to retain these beliefs, motivating them to imitate these beliefs, which they easily have the physical and mental potential to do.

Shows enculturation, as children can absorb the beliefs/norms of their culture (in this case, gender stereotypes) by simply observing and paying attention to models, regardless of whether or not they know them personally.

Paper 1::Sociocultural::SAQs.title: Explain one study of acculturation.
Paper 1::Sociocultural::SAQs.content:
Introduction


Acculturation is when people move to other cultures and start adopting the norms and behaviours of the majority culture.

According to Matsumoto, culture is “the set of attitudes, values, beliefs, and behaviours shared by a group of people, but different for each individual, communicated from one generation to the next.”

People have many reasons to move to a new culture, such as refuge, travel, or for work. There are four main acculturation strategies, proposed by Berry:

Assimilation: One abandons their original culture in favour for the new culture’s behaviours and norms.

Integration: One adopts the behaviour and norms of the new culture while maintaining their original culture.

Separation: One maintains their original culture while avoiding the new culture as much as possible.

Marginalization: One is unable to maintain their original culture, yet it is impossible for them to integrate into the new culture due to discrimination.

As humans are social beings with the need to fit in, the feeling of not being in the group leads to acculturative stress, the psychological somatic, or social difficulties that come with acculturation, often resulting in depression, anxiety, and other stresses.

Assimilation often leads to less acculturative stress than separation or marginalization.

Protective factors decrease acculturative stress and increase the likelihood of successful assimilation, while risk factors increase acculturative stress and decrease the likelihood of successful assimilation.

Generational gaps known as acculturation gaps occur when an immigrant parent has offspring in the new culture, with the child assimilating into the new culture more quickly and easily than the parent.

Study: Lueck & Wilson


Less acculturation gaps and a better relationship with family lead to less acculturative stress, and discrimination/negative moments happened to increase acculturative stress.

More positive outcomes from immigrants thus leaves people less stressed, and thus more likely to assimilate into the new culture.
